coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots displaying dual-wavelength photoluminescence and electrochemiluminescence prepared by high-energy ball milling
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Lve
Chen, Xu
Lu, Yanluo
Liu, Changxia
Yang, Wensheng
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with multiple surface states were prepared by high-energy ball milling a mixture of activated carbon and KOH, followed by ultrafiltration purification. The as-prepared CQDs exhibit dual-wavelength photoluminescence emission peaks. Most interestingly, the first emission peak is independent of excitation wavelength (λ ex) and the second one is dependent on λ ex and red shifts monotonically with increasing λ ex. Meanwhile, the as-prepared CQDs also present dual-wavelength electrochemiluminescence property.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagrams of (a) ECL responses and (b) ECL spectrum measurement devices. RC: reaction cell; W: working electrode; C: counter electrode; R: reference electrode; PMT: photomultiplier tube; F: filter.

HRTEM images of CQDs at (a) low and (b) high magnification. (c) FT-IR spectrum, (d) C 1s XPS analysis, (e) XRD pattern and (f) UV–Vis absorption spectrum of CQDs. The inset in (a) is the size distribution of CQDs. The insets in (b) are two representative images of individual CQDs with graphite structure (upper) and surface defect sites (lower). The insets in (f) are optical photographs of an aqueous solution of CQDs taken under visible light (left) and 365nm UV light (right).
(a) PL spectra of CQDs at 250< λ ex <530nm. (b) The up-converted PL spectra of CQDs at 550< λ ex <690nm.

(a) UV–Vis absorption spectra of CQDs (black line) and rCQDs (red line). (b) PL spectra of rCQDs at 250< λ ex <330nm. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
(a) HRTEM image and (b) size distribution of CQDs (3–10kDa), and (c) HRTEM image and (d) size distribution of CQDs (10–30kDa).
PL spectra of (a) CQDs (3–10kDa) and (b) CQDs (10–30kDa) at 250< λ ex <330nm.
(a) PL spectra of rCQDs at 410< λ ex <530nm. (b) PL spectra of CQDs (3–10kDa) and CQDs (10–30kDa) at 450< λ ex <490nm.

(a) Cyclic voltammogram and corresponding ECL potential curves of PBS blank (black lines) and PBS containing 0.2mgmL−1 CQDs (red lines). (b) ECL responses of the CQDs in the absence (red line) and presence (green line) of 10mmolL−1 K2S2O8. Inset: ECL response of the CQD-K2S2O8 system over ten cycles. (c) ECL spectrum obtained for the CQD-K2S2O8 system. The applied potential was cycled between −1.5 and +1.3V (vs. Ag/AgCl) at a scan rate of 0.1Vs−1. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots displaying dual-wavelength photoluminescence and electrochemiluminescence prepared by high-energy ball milling

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Chemical Resource Engineering, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing 100029, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Chemical Resource Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Life Science and Technology, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing 100029, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Life Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with multiple surface states were prepared by high-energy ball milling a mixture of activated carbon and KOH, followed by ultrafiltration purification. The as-prepared CQDs exhibit dual-wavelength photoluminescence emission peaks. Most interestingly, the first emission peak is independent of excitation wavelength (λ ex) and the second one is dependent on λ ex and red shifts monotonically with increasing λ ex. Meanwhile, the as-prepared CQDs also present dual-wavelength electrochemiluminescence property.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are carbon nanocrystals with a diameter of less than 10nm [1], and are a rising star in the nanocarbon family. CQDs have recently attracted considerable attention because of their fascinating merits, including low cytotoxicity, good biocompatibility, excellent solubility, good chemical inertness, high mobility, resistance to photobleaching and tunable band gap [2]. In particular, the unique photoluminescence (PL) and electrochemiluminescence (ECL) properties of CQDs make them promising in biomedicine, sensor, optronics and catalysis [3–8].

Tremendous effort has been expended to develop synthetic methods to prepare CQDs with excellent PL and/or ECL properties. These methods can be classified into top-down and bottom-up approaches [2]. The top-down methods involve breaking large carbon-based materials into nanosized CQDs, and include chemical ablation [9] and electrochemical oxidation [10–12]. Meanwhile, the bottom-up approaches fabricate CQDs using organic molecules as a carbon source, and include solution chemistry [13] and microwave synthesis [14,15]. The surface properties of CQDs, such as surface defect [16], surface group [17], surface passivation [14] and edge configuration [18] strongly influence their PL and ECL performance. Some strategies to tune surface chemistry, such as surface functionalization [13] and doping [19], have been used to improve the PL properties of CQDs. Nevertheless, the essential relationships between the surface and PL of CQDs, especially ECL, are still unclear. In addition, few synthetic approaches endow CQDs with ECL activity, although electrochemical oxidation [11] and microwave synthesis [14,15] are able to achieve this. Therefore, a facile synthetic route to prepare CQDs with tunable surface states and excellent ECL properties on a large scale remains to be developed.

Ball milling, which induces mechanochemical reactions, has been widely applied in materials synthesis [20]. Ball milling can produce nanomaterials on a large scale using cheap, easily obtained starting materials. More importantly, ball milling can cause a large number of oxygen-containing functional groups (OCFGs) to form on the surfaces of nanocarbon materials [20,21]. For example, single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWNTs) can be directly modified with numerous hydroxyl groups by ball milling a mixture of SWNTs and KOH [20]. More recently, Jeon et al. [21] fabricated edge-carboxylated graphene nanosheets simply by ball milling pristine graphite and dry ice. However, the synthesis of CQDs via ball milling has not been reported.

Herein, we report a facile approach to prepare CQDs with multiple surface states by high-energy ball milling (HEBM) a mixture of activated carbon (AC) and KOH, followed by ultrafiltration purification. The as-prepared CQDs exhibit dual-wavelength PL, where the first emission peak is independent of excitation wavelength (λ ex) and the second one is not, as well as dual-wavelength ECL.

Wood based activated carbon (AC) powder (200-mesh) was purchased from Chengde Xingyuan activated carbon Co., Ltd. (China). 3, 10 and 30kDa molecular weight cut off (MWCO) membranes (Amicon Ultra-15, Millipore) was obtained from Beijing Lablead trade Co., Ltd. (China). Other chemicals were analytical grade and used without further purification. Deionized (DI) water was used throughout the experiments. Phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) (pH 7.0, 0.1molL−1) was prepared using 0.1molL−1 Na2HPO4 and 0.1molL−1 NaH2PO4.

CQDs were prepared as follows: a mixture of AC and KOH (6g, mass ratio of 1:2) and stainless-steel balls (nine with a diameter of 20mm, twenty with a diameter of 10mm, and fifty with a diameter of 6mm) were placed in a stainless-steel vessel with a capacity of 0.1L. The balls were rotated at a rate of 500rpm for 50h by HEBM in a ball mill (Nanjing University Instrument Plant, QM-3SP2). An aliquot (0.6g) of the above mixture was added to DI water (30mL) and a uniform suspension was formed by mild ultrasonication for 10min. The suspension was neutralized with nitric acid and subsequently centrifuged at 9000×g for 10min to remove nonfluorescent deposits. The supernatant was then reduced with sodium borohydride (0.1molL−1) at 90°C for 6h; this step was optional. The supernatant was separated by a centrifugal filter device containing a membrane with a molecular weight cut off (MWCO) of 30kDa, and then the filtrate was separated by a centrifugal filter device with a MWCO membrane of 3kDa to obtain the components with an MW of 3–30kDa. The intercept was added to DI water (6×10mL) to remove inorganic salts and low-molecular-weight organic fluorophores and obtain CQDs. Unless otherwise indicated, CQDs mean the components purified by centrifugal filter devices with the MWCO membranes of 3–30kDa. The obtained CQD solutions were homogeneous and did not exhibit any agglomeration or precipitation at least for 9months when stored at 4°C. As the innate features of CQDs, the quantum yield of the prepared CQDs is 7.6% (Table S1 and a detailed description on quantum yield measurement are provided in ESI†), which is comparable to previous reports [14,22].

To study the effect of particle size on PL properties of the CQDs, we also used a membrane with a MWCO of 10kDa to further separate the CQDs. The filtrate and intercept are labeled as CQDs (3–10kDa) and CQDs (10–30kDa), respectively.

Elemental analysis for C, N, H and O was carried out using an Elementar Vario EL (Thermo Quest EA1112). Elemental analysis for Fe and K was performed by inductively coupled plasma-atomic emission spectroscopy (ICP-AES) (SPECTRO ARCOS EOP). High-resolution transmission electron microscopic (HRTEM) images were recorded on an electronic microscope (JEOL JEM-2100F 200KV). The infrared (IR) spectrum of dried CQDs was obtained on a Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrophotometer (Bruker Vertex 70V) using KBr pressed disks. Raman spectroscopy was performed with a confocal Raman spectrometer with a 514nm excitation laser and laser spot size of 0.5μm (Renishaw RM 2000). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was conducted using X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo ESCALAB 250) with Al Kα as radiation source and the C 1s peak at 284.8eV as an internal standard. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were collected on a X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku D/MAX 2500 VB2+/PC) with Cu Kα as radiation radiation. UV–Vis absorption was characterized by a UV–Vis-NIR spectrophotometer (Perkin-Elmer Lambda 35). All fluorescent spectra of prepared CQDs and rCQDs were obtained by a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Shimadzu RF-5301PC) equipped with a 1cm quartz cell at room temperature.

ECL and electrochemical signals were measured on an ECL detection system (Remex Electronic Instrument Ltd., Co., Xi’an, China, MPI-E) equipped with an ECL reaction cell. As illustrated in the schematic diagram in Fig. 1 a, the ECL responses of the as-prepared CQDs were studied in pH 7.0 PBS using a Pt sheet (an area of 0.5cm2) working electrode, a Pt sheet (an area of 0.5cm2) counter electrode and an Ag/AgCl reference electrode. The working and reference electrodes were located over the photomultiplier tube (PMT), while the counter electrode was positioned beside the PMT to remove the possible interfering light emitted from the counter electrode during the ECL measurements. The potential applied to the working electrode was cycled between −1.5 and +1.3V at a scan rate of 0.1Vs−1, and the voltage of the PMT was set at 800V.

To acquire the ECL spectrum, 18 single-wavelength transmission filters with wavelengths of 425, 440, 460, 475, 490, 505, 520, 535, 555, 575, 590, 620, 640, 665, 690, 715, 740 and 765nm with a half width of 12nm (Beijing Institute of Technology) were positioned at the bottom of the ECL reaction cell so that they could cover the whole PMT, as shown in Fig. 1b.

The HRTEM image of the CQDs in Fig. 2 a shows that the CQDs are monodisperse and lamellar nanoparticles. The majority of particles have a diameter in the range of 1.6–2.8nm with an average size of 2.2nm, as shown in the inset of Fig. 2a. A magnified HRTEM image (Fig. 2b) clearly reveals lattice planes with a spacing of 0.208nm (upper inset of Fig. 2b), which is consistent with that of the in-plane lattice spacing of graphite (100 facet) [19]. The CQDs exhibit surface defective sites (lower inset of Fig. 2b) like previously reported CQDs [16], which suggests that the CQDs have multiple surface states. Further evidence for this speculation is provided by Raman, FT-IR, XPS, XRD and UV–Vis absorption spectral measurements of the CQDs. The Raman spectrum of the CQDs (Fig. S2 in ESI†) reveals the D and G bands at 1380 and 1600cm−1 with a large intensity ratio I D/I G =2.18. The high I D/I G value illustrates that the CQDs is highly disordered, which ascribe to the multiple surface states. The FT-IR spectrum of the CQDs in Fig. 2c confirms the presence of abundant OCFGs, as evidenced by the stretching vibrations of –OH groups at 3400cm−1, –CO groups at 1740cm−1 and –COOH/–COOK groups at 1580 and 1380cm−1. The 1580 and 1380cm−1 bands can be regarded as the G and D bands of carbon materials, where symmetry breaking makes these bands IR-active like the literature [23] reported. The high-resolution XPS C 1s spectrum of the CQDs (Fig. 2d) exhibits three main peaks at 284.8eV (sp2 C–C), 285.7eV (–C–O) and 288.2eV (–CO), also indicating the presence of numerous OCFGs. In their XRD pattern, the CQDs exhibit a broad peak (002 facet) centered at around 21.5° and interlayer spacing of 0.403nm (Fig. 2e), which is wider than that of graphite structure. This can be attributed to OCFGs increasing the interlayer distance of the CQDs [22]. The CQDs show a broad absorption below 550nm without any other obvious peak (Fig. 2f), consistent with the CQDs containing many OCFGs [19]. Photographs of the dispersed CQDs under visible and 365nm UV light are shown in the insets of Fig. 2f. The bright greenish yellow luminescence of the CQDs is strong enough to be easily seen with the naked eye. From the elemental analysis result (Table S2 in ESI†), the content of oxygen element is 30.77wt.%, which also suggests the existence of abundant OCFGs in the CQDs. In addition, there is trace iron element (0.07wt.%) existing in the CQDs, which is unavoidable from the preparation method of HEBM. However, trace iron has barely influence on the ECL intensity (Fig. S1 in ESI†).

The PL behavior of the CQDs was systematically investigated by changing the λ ex from 250 to 530nm at intervals of 20nm, as shown in Fig. 3 a. The dual emission of the CQDs is clearly seen as a wide emission band at 290< λ ex <390nm that is composed of two overlapping emission peaks. Most interestingly, the first emission peak is independent of λ ex with its maximum at a fixed emission wavelength (λ em) of 490nm. Meanwhile, the second peak is dependent on λ ex and red shift monotonically with increasing λ ex, and the maximum λ em is 540nm at λ ex =470nm.

At λ ex from 550 to 690nm, the CQDs show remarkable up-conversion PL (Fig. 3b). The up-converted PL from the CQDs should arise from a multiphoton active process [24]. Similar to the down-conversion PL spectra, the up-conversion PL spectra also exhibit dual emission. The first emission peak is independent of λ ex with its maximum at a fixed λ em of 490nm, while the second is dependent on λ ex. CQDs with such up-conversion PL properties may be an ideal candidate for bioscience and energy technology [24].

To further elucidate the dual-wavelength PL mechanism of the CQDs, the CQDs were reduced by NaBH4. The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of the reduced CQDs (rCQDs) shows a strong absorption peak at 270nm, and no appreciable absorption peak is observed for the original CQDs (Fig. 4 a). This change of absorption behavior can be attributed to chemical reduction modifying the surface states of the CQDs [25]. Meanwhile, as λ ex is increased from 250 to 330nm, the first PL emission peak remains independent of λ ex but has its maximum located at 463nm (Fig. 4b), which is a blue shift of 27nm compared with that of the original CQDs (Fig. 3a). This indicates that the λ ex-independent peak is mainly related to the surface states of the CQDs controlled by the OCFGs [12,17].

The CQDs were further separated into CQDs (3–10kDa) and CQDs (10–30kDa), which have mean particle sizes of 1.8 and 2.4nm, respectively (Fig. 5 ). The position of the first PL emission peak for CQDs (3–10kDa) and CQDs (10–30kDa) hardly changed (Fig. 6 ), which further illustrates that the first PL emission peak is related to surface states rather than the particle size of the CQDs. Comparing the PL spectra of the CQDs (Fig. 3a) and rCQDs (Fig. 7 a) at 410< λ ex <530nm reveals that the spectra of these two kinds of CQDs are almost the same. This confirms that the second peak, which is dependent on λ ex, is not related to the surface states of the CQDs. However, the influence of the particle size of the CQDs on the position of the second PL peak is not negligible. The strongest emission peak of the CQDs (3–10kDa) appears at λ ex =450nm, whereas the strongest emission peak of the CQDs (10–30kDa) is at λ ex =470nm (Fig. 7b). Therefore, the second PL peak of the CQDs is speculated to originate from the carbogenic core [13,17], which is influenced by the particle size of the CQDs [10].

ECL, in which a light-emitting chemiluminescent reaction is preceded by an electrochemical reaction, has been widely used in fundamental studies and analytical applications [26]. CQDs have received considerable interest as a kind of “green” luminophore that display ECL. Accordingly, the ECL behavior of the as-prepared CQDs was studied. As shown in Fig.8 a, when the potential is cycled between −1.5 and +1.3V (vs. Ag/AgCl), an intense ECL signal from the CQDs is detected in the negative potential region, and a relatively weak ECL signal is observed in the positive region. No ECL signal is observed in either region in the absence of CQDs. This implies that the CQDs prepared by the HEBM method possess excellent ECL activity.

When 10mmolL−1 K2S2O8, a common cathodic coreactant (or hole donor) used in ECL systems, is added to the system, the ECL response of cathode increases obviously (Fig. 8b). Moreover, the strong cathodic ECL signal is very stable when the potential is cycled between −1.5 and +1.3V (see the inset of Fig. 8b). Most interestingly, as shown in Fig. 8c, the ECL spectrum contains two emission peaks located at 570 and 645nm. Compared with the dual-wavelength PL spectrum, the two ECL emission peaks are both red shifted, indicating that the ECL emission is more sensitive and dependent on the surface states of the CQDs than PL, which is in accordance with the literature [8]. The HRTEM (lower inset of Fig. 2b), FT-IR (Fig. 2c) and XPS (Fig. 2d) confirm the presence of two kinds of surface states of the CQDs, that is, surface defect and OCFGs. Accordingly, the CQDs present dual-wavelength ECL property. Due to both of the ECL emission peaks are red shifted in comparison to PL, we speculate that the first ECL emission peaks is arise from the surface defect, and the second one is related to the OCFGs.

In summary, CQDs with unique dual-wavelength PL and ECL properties were prepared by an HEBM method. This economical, simple and efficient method allows the potential for large-scale production of CQDs. The OCFGs on the surface of the CQDs endowed them excellent water dispersibility, which coupled with the size-dependent emission originating from the carbogenic core, led to dual-wavelength PL property. Because of the presence of two kinds of surface states, the as-prepared CQDs also exhibited dual-wavelength ECL. The unique dual-wavelength PL and ECL properties of these CQDs make them promising in biomedicine, sensor, optronics and catalysis.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was funded by the National Basic Research Program of China (Grant No. 2014CB932103), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51272020, 21236003), the Research Fund for the Doctoral Program of Higher Education of China (20120010110013), and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (YS1406).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The quantum yield measurement, elemental analysis and effect of iron on electrochemiluminescence, and Raman and XPS spectra of the CQDs are provided in the supplementary information (SI). Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2015.06.084.
